programming.name: Programming Tools
programming.description: General purpose creative coding frameworks and platforms

creative-libraries.name: Creative Libraries
creative-libraries.description: Libraries that extend creative coding capabilities

physical-computing.name: Physical Computing
physical-computing.description: Hardware and software for physical interactions

audio-visual.name: Audio-Visual
audio-visual.description: Tools focused on audio and visual performance

multimedia-authoring.name: Multimedia Authoring
multimedia-authoring.description: Interactive-media and live-performance authoring environments — visual, timeline, or patch-based tools for making interactive media, often with their own scripting languages.

Processing.description: Java-based creative coding language and environment

releases.notes: Casey Reas and Ben Fry begin Processing at the MIT Media Lab's Aesthetics + Computation Group, extending John Maeda's Design By Numbers. Early alpha releases circulated under the name 'Proce55ing'.
Public beta releases (revisions 0090+) circulated from 2005 ahead of the 1.0 launch.

P5.js.description: JavaScript library for creative coding on the web, created by Lauren McCarthy (with Evelyn Eastmond) beginning in 2013 as a reimagining of Processing for the browser, with access and inclusion held as core design goals. First public beta 2014; version 1.0 in 2020.

releases.notes: Preliminary Alpha Release
First Public Beta
Major Release
Major Release, WebGL overhaul
Major Release, YUIDocs and linting overhaul, Shader support
Major Release, core file restructure, developer_docs refactor

Max/MSP.description: Visual programming language for music and multimedia

releases.notes: Was Max/MSP (first Cycling '74 release) the same as Max 2?

Pure Data.description: Open-source visual programming language for real-time audio and multimedia, created by Miller Puckette as an open alternative to Max/MSP. Pd is versioned 0.x and has never reached 1.0.

releases.notes: Miller Puckette; presented at ICMC 1996
Binary file creation dates
First release?
First workshop releases by Zach Lieberman, Theo Watson and Arturo Castro. oF has never had a 1.0 — the toolkit is still on the 0.x line; its official versions page dates v0.01 to 2007.
Related documentation

FilterFactory.description: Photoshop plugin for creating custom filters

releases.notes: This was a plugin that was released with Photoshop 3.0 which allowed to write filters using a very limited instruction set. In hindsight one could say it was the predecessor or what ShaderToy is now. Around this plugin a very active community of coders had gathered in a mailing list called "The Filter Factory Discussion Group" which was originally started on Compuserve in 1995. During the ~4 years in which the group was most active thousands of experimental filters were shared that went beyond basic color manipulations but rather tried to push the language and memory limits that the plugin imposed. Lots of the code shared I would definitely call "Generative Art".
Mac-only debut at Apple WWDC
Terrain engine, dynamic shadows, DirectX; iPhone support in 2008
Android support, deferred rendering; Asset Store era
Mecanim animation system, DirectX 11
Real-time global illumination, WebGL, free Personal tier
Date-based versioning; Timeline and Cinemachine
Scriptable Render Pipeline; HDRP/URP preview, Shader Graph
Burst Compiler; URP out of preview
URP/HDRP matured; long-term-support baseline
Return to major versioning; Muse and Sentis AI tools
Debuted in the game Unreal; licensed to studios, not sold standalone
Hardware rendering; PS2/Xbox/GameCube support
HDR, multithreading, DirectX 9; widely licensed
Free UE3 SDK opened the engine to indie developers
$19/mo full-source subscription; Blueprint visual scripting
Engine made free with a 5% royalty model
Cloud tool for photoreal, fully-rigged digital humans
First public Nanite geometry and Lumen global illumination
Full release; Nanite, Lumen, MetaHuman production-ready

TouchDesigner.description: Visual development platform for real-time media

releases.notes: First Arduino boards; the Arduino IDE 1.0 came later, in 2011
First public release, by the MESO group
Switched rendering to DirectX 9; shown at transmediale
C# plugin interface opens node development to third parties
New CodeEditor, PluginInterfaces V2
10-year anniversary release; adds 64-bit builds
Ships the VL visual-programming editor; groundwork for gamma
First stable release of the from-scratch VL-based rewrite
Among the last releases of the classic beta line

Cables.gl.description: Visual programming environment for the web

A-Frame.description: Web framework for building VR experiences

ML5.js.description: Friendly machine learning for the web. Community-driven library begun at NYU's ITP (from Cristóbal Valenzuela's p5deeplearn, developed under Daniel Shiffman), inspired by the Processing Foundation's accessibility mission. First beta (0.1.0) 2018.

releases.notes: First beta

Vuo.description: Visual programming environment for interactive media

Sonic Pi.description: Code-based music creation and performance tool created by Sam Aaron at the University of Cambridge with the Raspberry Pi Foundation, originally to support computing and music teaching in schools; built on the SuperCollider engine and also used for algorave live coding.

ESP32.description: Low-cost system on a chip microcontroller

ARKit.description: Augmented reality development platform for iOS

ARCore.description: Augmented reality development platform for Android

OpenCV.description: Computer vision and machine learning library

Vega.description: Visualization grammar for creating interactive visualizations

Observable.description: JavaScript notebook for exploring data and creating interactive visualizations

SuperCollider.description: Platform for audio synthesis and algorithmic composition, released in 1996 by James McCartney and open-sourced under the GPL in 2002. Widely used for live coding and algorave, and the synthesis engine behind Sonic Pi and TidalCycles.

Director.description: Macromedia (later Adobe) Director — a multimedia authoring application scripted with the Lingo language, descended from MacroMind VideoWorks; the dominant tool for CD-ROM and interactive-kiosk media through the 1990s.

releases.notes: First release under the Director name, descended from MacroMind VideoWorks. Ship year commonly cited as 1988 (sources vary 1987–1989). Confidence: approximate.
Introduced the Lingo scripting language.

Flash / Animate.description: The vector animation and interactivity authoring tool that began as FutureSplash Animator, became Macromedia Flash (with the ActionScript language), and was renamed Adobe Animate in 2016.

releases.notes: Original release by FutureWave Software, before the Macromedia acquisition.
FutureWave acquired by Macromedia Dec 1996; product rebranded Macromedia Flash.
Renamed from Flash Professional (Feb 8 2016). The Flash Player runtime reached end-of-life Dec 31 2020, but the authoring tool continues as Animate.

Quartz Composer.description: Apple's node-based visual programming environment for processing and rendering graphical data, shipped with the Mac OS X developer tools and widely used for VJ and motion-graphics prototyping.

releases.notes: First shipped in the Mac OS X 10.4 developer tools, based on Pierre-Olivier Latour's PixelShox (acquired by Apple 2002).

Isadora.description: TroikaTronix Isadora — a node-based graphic programming environment by Mark Coniglio for real-time manipulation of video and interactive media in live performance.

releases.notes: By Mark Coniglio / Troika Ranch; some secondary sources cite 2002. Confidence: year.

Scratch.description: MIT Media Lab's block-based visual programming language and online community, designed to teach coding by snapping graphical command blocks together.

releases.notes: Public launch by the Lifelong Kindergarten group (Mitchel Resnick et al.).

Nodebox.description: A generative-design environment from Frederik De Bleser and Tom De Smedt (EMRG, Sint Lucas Antwerpen). One continuous project across a full rewrite: NodeBox 1 (2002) was a Mac-only app that generated 2D visuals from Python code via Quartz/PyObjC, while NodeBox 3 - the releases from 2017 on - is a cross-platform Java rewrite built around a visual node graph rather than a code editor. Recorded as a single lineage, in the same way Processing spans its own rewrites; the Python and node-graph eras are quite different products sharing a team, a name, and a purpose.

releases.notes: Original Mac NodeBox, based on DrawBot; 'online since 2002' per the project. Confidence: approximate.

Scriptographer.description: Jürg Lehni's scripting plugin that let users extend and automate Adobe Illustrator with JavaScript to build tools and generative drawings; direct predecessor to Paper.js.

releases.notes: Developed for Adobe Illustrator 9/10; exact first-release date not precisely documented. Discontinued when Illustrator CS6 (2012) removed the ADM API it relied on; succeeded by Paper.js (2011). Confidence: approximate.

Basil.js.description: An open-source scripting library that brings Processing-style computational and generative design to Adobe InDesign, from the Basel School of Design (HGK FHNW).

releases.notes: Released under an MIT license; initiated by Benedikt Groß with Ludwig Zeller, Ted Davis, and others.

StructureSynth.description: Mikael Hvidtfeldt Christensen's application that generates 3D structures from a rule-based 'eisenscript' grammar, inspired by Structured Vector Graphics and Context Free Art.

releases.notes: Initially released as Windows binaries; open source (GPL/LGPL).

Papervision3D.description: An open-source real-time 3D engine for Adobe Flash Player, written in ActionScript, that brought interactive 3D to the Flash web platform.

releases.notes: By Carlos Ulloa; open-sourced by end of 2006. Final release 2.1.932 (Dec 2009); superseded by Away3D as Flash declined.

Logo.description: Educational programming language created at Bolt, Beranek and Newman (BBN) to make programming concepts tangible to children. Its turtle graphics - drawing by commanding a relative-motion agent rather than plotting absolute coordinates - became the founding metaphor for learning to code by drawing, and the ancestor of Design By Numbers, Processing, and Scratch.

releases.notes: Designed by Wally Feurzeig, Seymour Papert, and Cynthia Solomon. Created at BBN in Cambridge, Massachusetts - not at MIT, despite Papert's MIT association; the work later migrated to the MIT Logo Laboratory. Papert is the famous name but is one of three co-designers.

Added by Papert in the late 1960s; 1969 is approximate, as sources date it only to 'the late 1960s'. Mike Paterson wrote the first Turtle Specification in August 1969, and both a floor turtle and a display turtle were in use by the 1970-71 school year.

Design By Numbers.description: John Maeda's deliberately minimal programming environment from the MIT Media Lab's Aesthetics + Computation Group, built to teach computational design to visual designers with no programming background. Its severe constraints - a fixed 101x101 pixel canvas (coordinates 0-100) and grayscale only - made it the direct precursor to Processing, which Maeda's students Casey Reas and Ben Fry built after concluding those limits were the essence of the platform and shouldn't simply be extended.

releases.notes: Software and the MIT Press book were both released in 1999; the 2001 date cited in some sources is the paperback reissue, not the first edition. Canvas fixed at 101x101 pixels (x and y run 0-100 inclusive) with gray values 0-100 and no color. First workshop taught 25 August 1999 at AIGA headquarters.

Final version; the third version was written by Ben Fry, with Casey Reas designing the courseware and Tom White also contributing. The Aesthetics + Computation Group closed the same year. Reimplemented in JavaScript by Lingdong Huang as 'Design By Numbers Again' (https://www.media.mit.edu/projects/design-by-numbers-again/overview/).

ACU.description: An internal, never publicly released C++ graphics and media toolkit built inside the MIT Media Lab's Aesthetics + Computation Group, and the group's everyday production tool for computational art - running on SGI Octane and O2 workstations in the late 1990s, then on commodity PCs by 2000. A direct precursor to both Processing, which took its 'high ceiling' ambition from ACU, and openFrameworks, which was created in response to ACU being neither open source nor well maintained enough to share and teach with.

releases.notes: Date is APPROXIMATE and set by project-participant testimony rather than a document: no founding date is recorded anywhere. The archived repo is explicitly a 'partial history' - git commits run Jul 1999 to Nov 2001, but its README changelog reaches back to 25 Jan 1999 and already describes additions to an existing library, so ACU demonstrably predates Jan 1999. Started by Ben Fry, with significant contributions from Tom White and Jared Schiffman; used across the group, with Casey Reas appearing as a user and documenter rather than an author.

End of the partial git history, not necessarily the end of use: ACU was reportedly still in use as late as 2002. Effectively ended with the Aesthetics + Computation Group, which ran 1996-2003. openFrameworks 0.01 followed in August 2005.

Wiring.description: Open-source electronics prototyping platform - a language, a Processing-derived IDE, and a microcontroller board - created by Hernando Barragan as his Master's thesis at the Interaction Design Institute Ivrea, supervised by Massimo Banzi and Casey Reas. It is the direct ancestor of Arduino, which forked its source, language and IDE, and the bridge that carried Processing's sketch-and-run model into physical computing: setup()/loop(), digitalWrite() and pinMode() are Barragan's design.

releases.notes: Thesis completed 2004; 25 boards hand-ordered March 2004, ~100 manufactured by IDII late 2004 and used to teach the 'Strangely Familiar' course that autumn. Dated to 2004 because that is when Wiring existed and was taught with. English Wikipedia's 'initial release 2007' is flatly wrong - contradicted by the project's own release log and Barragan's account.

Earliest release on the official update log. Release #05 (2005-09-12) is when Wiring went open source. Arduino forked Wiring in 2005, adding support for the cheaper ATmega8; per Barragan, no one asked him first, and he was never invited onto the Arduino team. In 2013 David Mellis publicly acknowledged the Arduino team 'hadn't done a very good job acknowledging Wiring'. The fork is well documented; Barragan's account of the intent behind it is his own and Arduino has not published a detailed rebuttal.
Last release. Maintained at Universidad de Los Andes, Bogota; effectively dormant since, though never formally ended.

Fritzing.description: Open-source EDA tool that lets designers document a breadboard prototype and turn it into a real PCB, built around a breadboard-view metaphor rather than an engineer's schematic. Explicitly modelled on Processing and Arduino - the aim was to do for circuit design what they did for code and electronics - and its breadboard diagrams became the visual lingua franca of maker-culture documentation.

releases.notes: First public release. The project began August 2007 at the Interaction Design Lab, FH Potsdam, under a Brandenburg state grant. Sources dating it 2009 are citing the TEI '09 paper, not the release; GitHub is a red herring (repo created 2014, migrated from Google Code).
Development stalled 2016-2019 as revenue declined; Aisler assembled a dev team from 2019 and made a donation mandatory for binaries, though the source stayed GPL.

ChucK.description: Strongly-timed, concurrent audio programming language built at Princeton's Sound Lab for on-the-fly synthesis, composition and performance. It made live coding of audio legible to the academic computer-music world and seeded the laptop-orchestra practice (PLOrk/SLOrk); it was also the substrate for Wekinator's original implementation.

releases.notes: A public alpha was downloadable in 2003 - the Nov 2003 snapshot of chuck.cs.princeton.edu reads 'version: alpha 1.0' with a live download link. The language itself dates from 2002. Descended from Perry Cook's STK unit generators; conceptually a reaction to Max/MSP and SuperCollider.
The project's own VERSIONS log calls this the 'initial release' (Spring 2004) - i.e. the first numbered/stable release, as distinct from the 2003 alpha.

TidalCycles.description: Haskell-embedded language for live-coding algorithmic patterns, built on a functional representation of cyclic time rather than a timeline or step sequencer. The canonical algorave instrument and the origin of the 'pattern as a pure function of time' model that most later live-coding languages copied; makes no sound itself, driving SuperCollider via SuperDirt. Initiated by Alex McLean around 2009; McLean co-founded TOPLAP and co-coined the term 'algorave'.

releases.notes: Per the official history, McLean shared the code under a free licence and gave his first talk on it in 2009. Origins trace to a 2006 pattern-rotation DSL, sometimes miscited as the release date.
First packaged release, and the only exact machine-verifiable timestamp in Tidal's early history.

Hydra.description: Browser-based live-coding video synthesizer whose JavaScript API mimics analog modular video synthesis - oscillators, kaleidoscopes and webcam feeds chained and fed back into each other, compiled to WebGL. It made live-coded visuals frictionless (a URL, no install) and became the default visual counterpart to TidalCycles at algoraves. Created by Olivia Jack; it originated in a December 2017 workshop she gave at the International Conference on Live Coding, with roots in the Bogotá live-coding scene.

releases.notes: First public release. The repo dates from Nov 2017, which is why both '2017' and '2018' are cited - they are right about different things (project start vs release). Descended conceptually from analog video synthesis and from Charlie Roberts' Gibber.

Fluxus.description: Live-coding 3D graphics environment scripted in Scheme (later Racket), where the code editor is rendered transparently over the realtime scene it generates so code and output share one screen. The first widely used environment for live-coded 3D visuals, it established the on-screen-code performance aesthetic that Hydra and others inherited.

releases.notes: Corroborated by the first CVS commit, 25 Jan 2005 by Dave Griffiths. Performed with by slub (Griffiths + Alex McLean), the same circle that produced TidalCycles. Named after, but unrelated to, the 1960s Fluxus art movement.

Last release. Last sign of life anywhere is a commit of 2015-10-07; neither repo is formally archived, so there is no clean end event. Note: Extempore is NOT a Fluxus descendant - a common conflation; it comes from Sorensen's Impromptu lineage.

Pixi.js.description: 2D WebGL renderer with a seamless Canvas fallback, built so interactive content could 'build once, play everywhere'. It was the main technical landing pad for Flash refugees after the mobile Flash Player's death: it deliberately reproduced the Flash display list (Stage, Sprite, MovieClip, addChild) in JavaScript, and went on to power Phaser and a large share of HTML5 games and interactive web work.

releases.notes: Announced in 'Pixi.js released!', corroborated by inbound community PRs starting the next day. Do NOT date this from GitHub tags: the v1.0.0 tag was created retroactively in 2021 and would date Pixi 1.0 eight years late. npm publishing only starts in Jan 2014. Claims that Pixi was 'open sourced in 2015' are wrong.

Tone.js.description: Web Audio framework for making interactive music in the browser, providing DAW-like abstractions - a global transport, tempo-relative time, buses, synths and effects - on top of the raw Web Audio API. It is the library that made Web Audio musically usable, and underpins Chrome Music Lab, A.I. Duet and the Magenta music demos.

releases.notes: First tagged release; npm 'tone' 0.1.0 landed the same day. The project began 2014-03-11 and was not called Tone at first - early commits show a rename from 'WebAudio'/'AudioUnit'. Its lineage is primary-sourced: Mann's own Web Audio Conference paper credits Max/MSP metrical timing, Ableton Live's transport notation, and Max Mathews' MUSIC-N unit-generator/score split.

Two.js.description: Renderer-agnostic 2D drawing API exposing one identical API that renders interchangeably to SVG, Canvas2D or WebGL. Effectively the 2D counterpart to three.js - it took three.js's scene-graph and pluggable-renderer architecture and applied it to flat vector graphics; best known as the animation engine behind Patatap.

releases.notes: Repo pushed public 2012-09-05; first commit 2012-08-11. There is no clean v1.0 launch announcement, hence month precision. Do NOT date from GitHub releases: the earliest three were bulk-created within two minutes of each other on 2014-01-08 and are backdated. Descended directly from three.js - the first alpha was literally built with it.

Context Free Art.description: Generative art program that renders images from a context-free design grammar (CFDG), where recursive rules over three primitives expand into images of millions of shapes. It popularised the design grammar as a creative-coding paradigm and is the direct model for Structure Synth, which credits it by name.

releases.notes: No exact release date is published; 2005 is taken from the repo's copyright headers ('CFDG Copyright 2005 Chris Coyne'). Coyne designed the grammar and wrote the original command-line version; Mark Lentczner - usually omitted from summaries - built the Mac GUI, and John Horigan wrote the Windows version and still maintains it. Still actively maintained despite an empty GitHub Releases tab; versions live as git tags.

Grasshopper.description: Node-based visual programming environment inside Rhinoceros 3D that lets designers build generative algorithms and parametric models by wiring components instead of writing code. It became the defining tool of computational and parametric architecture, and the template later node-based design environments answer to.

releases.notes: First released as a free Rhino 4 plugin under the name Explicit History, chosen to contrast with Rhino's native implicit History feature; later renamed Grasshopper. Weakest date in this batch: Rutten's own account confirms the name but no McNeel primary source fixes the month, so September 2007 rests on secondary sources.
Rhino 6 shipped with Grasshopper built in, ending its ~10-year run as a separate long-term beta plugin.

Godot.description: Free, MIT-licensed 2D/3D game engine with an integrated scene-based editor - the first fully open-source engine to reach genuine parity with commercial incumbents. Its open-sourcing turned a decade-old proprietary Argentine in-house engine into the default non-corporate option for indie and creative-coding game work.

releases.notes: Released under the MIT licence by OKAM. The engine has a decade of closed pre-history (codenamed Larvotor, begun c.2001 and commercially licensed right up to the open-sourcing), but 2014 is when it began to exist publicly.
First stable public build. Adoption accelerated sharply after Unity's 2023 runtime-fee controversy.

Wekinator.description: Software that lets artists and musicians build interactive systems by demonstrating human actions and desired computer responses - training machine-learning models by example rather than writing code. It established interactive machine learning for creative practice as a genre, and its train-by-demonstration model is the conceptual ancestor of ml5.js, Runway ML and Teachable Machine.

releases.notes: Built on the Weka toolkit (hence the name), out of Fiebrink's Princeton PhD with Perry Cook and Dan Trueman. Originally two components - one in ChucK, one in Java - talking over OSC, which made it host-agnostic: it drives Max/MSP, Processing, openFrameworks, Ableton, Unity, Quartz Composer and Arduino.

Full rewrite that dropped the ChucK dependency for generic OSC. STATUS IS CONTESTED: the project site says it is still gently updated, but the last release is July 2016 and the last commit February 2021. Recorded as active to match the maintainer's own claim; treat as effectively dormant.

toxiclibs.description: Extensive open-source library collection for computational design in Java/Processing - geometry, physics, colour, simulation, volumetrics - written by artist Karsten Schmidt. The canonical example of the Processing ecosystem's second layer, where power users built the advanced toolkits Processing core deliberately omitted.

releases.notes: Date is approximate: source copyright headers run 2006-2011 and no first-release announcement survives. Ported outward to JavaScript (toxiclibs.js) and Ruby. Note toxiclibs.org is DEAD - the domain lapsed and now serves an unrelated gambling site, so the GitHub repo is the canonical link.
Last substantive release; a promised final release never shipped and the repo has been dormant since ~2013. Dan Shiffman has published a Processing 3.0+ compatible build.

Pixera.description: Real-time media server by Austrian manufacturer AV Stumpfl for driving projection and LED shows, with content management, timeline playback, and projection/pixel mapping.

releases.notes: v1.0 announced Jan 2019 (first shown at InfoComm 2018)

disguise.description: Show-production platform (formerly d3) combining a real-time 3D media server with software and hardware, used for large-scale concert tours, projection mapping, and xR/LED-volume virtual production. Lineage traces to United Visual Artists.

releases.notes: d3 Technologies founded 2010 (Ash Nehru / UVA lineage); renamed 'disguise' in 2017

HyperCard.description: Stack-based multimedia authoring environment ('software erector set') by Bill Atkinson at Apple, scripted with the HyperTalk language. It let non-programmers build interactive 'stacks' of cards linking text, graphics, and sound, and was a foundational influence on multimedia authoring and hypertext.

releases.notes: Released at MacWorld Boston; later bundled free with Macs

ComfyUI.description: Node-based visual programming environment for building generative-AI image and video pipelines, wiring model, prompt, sampler, and post-processing nodes into a graph. One of the most widely used interfaces for Stable Diffusion and later open diffusion models.

releases.notes: Node-based Stable Diffusion UI by comfyanonymous

BASIC Stamp.description: Early microcontroller module from Parallax with a small BASIC interpreter (PBASIC) in ROM, programmed from a PC over a simple connection. A staple of 1990s-2000s electronics hobbyists and education, and a widely used physical-computing platform well before Arduino.

releases.notes: Parallax; PBASIC interpreter in ROM
24-pin DIP form; more memory, faster, more I/O
